data.title: American Airlines Cuts 2026 Earnings Forecast Amid Rising Fuel Costs
data.description:
American Airlines (NASDAQ:AAL) has downgraded its profit projections for 2026, as fluctuating and elevated fuel prices continue to pressure the airline's financial performance. The latest forecast suggests the carrier could swing from a loss of 65 cents per share to a gain of 65 cents, a notable drop from prior estimates reflecting a range between a 40-cent deficit and $1.10 in earnings per share.

Fuel expenses remain a stubborn variable for carriers this year, even though American raised ticket prices to help absorb the cost hike. Despite solid demand, the surge in jet fuel prices - which are the highest single operational cost after labor - is complicating profit margins across the industry.

For the current quarter, American predicted adjusted losses between 70 cents and 10 cents per share, falling short of the 28 cents per share analysts anticipated. However, the company expects revenues to increase between 16% and 19%, overtopping the forecast growth of 16.6%. The airline plans to boost its flight capacity by up to 5% in the third quarter to meet travel demand.

CEO Robert Isom has publicly acknowledged the pressing need to narrow the profitability gap that has grown between American and its major competitors, Delta Air Lines and United Airlines. While no specific timeline was provided, he indicated the airline's long-term strategies include ordering new wide-body jets and refitting existing aircraft with more premium seating to enhance revenue potential.

Reflecting recent results, American reported a second quarter profit of $71 million, a steep 88% drop from the $599 million recorded a year earlier. Earnings per share settled at 11 cents, down from 91 cents in Q2 2025. Meanwhile, overall revenue climbed 16.3% to $16.74 billion during the period, driven in part by a 10% rise in passenger revenue per available seat mile, a key measure of pricing strength.

Adjusted earnings per share for the quarter came in at 15 cents, surpassing the 3 cents per share analysts expected. The numbers underscore the challenges that volatile fuel prices have posed to American's bottom line, even as elevated airfare and passenger volumes provide some relief.

The broader U.S. airline sector has been navigating similar headwinds. While airlines continue to benefit from strong travel demand post-pandemic, fuel costs remain a wildcard, keeping investors on edge. American's latest earnings revision underscores how sensitive airline profitability is to energy market swings, a dynamic unlikely to disappear anytime soon.

Whether American's plans to upgrade its fleet and add more lucrative premium seats will significantly shift its competitive position remains to be seen. Meanwhile, the airline industry is facing a balancing act-managing costs while responding to consumer willingness to absorb higher fares.

data.title: Alphabet Shares Drop After AI Spending Surge Sparks Analyst Divergence
data.description:
Alphabet's stock (NASDAQ:GOOG) slipped nearly 5% prior to Thursday's market open after the company revealed plans to ramp up spending on artificial intelligence, showing a bigger budget than expected. While the revenue headline was strong, with cloud business soaring over 80% compared to last year, the flagged capital expenditure range climbed from $180 billion-$190 billion to $195 billion-$205 billion, prompting some jitters.

Even with the pullback, Alphabet shares remain about 9% higher year to date, fueled largely by optimism surrounding Google Cloud's explosive expansion and the company's new Gemini AI technology integrated into search and developer tools. Yet, the heightened AI investment is also causing some investors to hesitate, concerned it might weigh on near-term earnings.

Google Earnings Call Transcript Summary of Q2 2026 >>

Analysts remain sharply split but generally positive on Alphabet's long-term prospects. Roth Capital's Rohit Kulkarni underscored worries about the hefty ongoing capital needs but deemed them necessary for tapping into the rapidly growing AI market. Roth maintains a 'buy' rating and a price target implying near 30% upside.

Citizens analyst Andrew Boone echoed this sentiment, highlighting that the crux isn't how much Alphabet spends on AI, but whether these investments yield commensurate returns. Boone's $515 target suggests a hefty 51% gain, banking on sustained elevated spending industry-wide rather than cutting back anytime soon.

On the more cautious side, JPMorgan (NYSE:JPM) trimmed its target to $420 but still tagged Alphabet as 'overweight,' noting the continued returns from AI efforts in cloud, search, and subscriptions. Evercore ISI raised revenue estimates slightly but also bumped free cash flow losses due to increased capex, acknowledging a moderate sell-off in the aftermarket despite strong core fundamentals.

Cantor lowered its price target to $420, keeping an optimistic bias about Alphabet's commitment to leading large language model development. Likewise, Citi's analyst Ronald Josey sees upside of roughly 31%, citing the AI investments driving gains across search and cloud segments.

Piper Sandler trimmed its target more modestly to $395, pointing out the need for a leading frontier AI model to justify valuations and eagerly awaiting upcoming Gemini model updates. Jefferies touted Alphabet's ad business, YouTube positioning, and promising cloud segment as multiple engines for growth, suggesting sizable call option-like opportunities across AI-related products.

RBC reiterated its outperform view but warned of a potential pause as the market digests cash burn and margin pressure from hefty compute infrastructure investments. Still, the combination of data muscle, cloud scale, and AI innovation appeared a strong bet on dominance. Pivotal Research raised its target to $475, accentuating Alphabet's advantage with its proprietary AI chip technology and massive distribution footprint, aiming to cement Gemini as the top AI platform worldwide.

The broad takeaway: while heavy AI investment might pressure earnings in the short term, major players including Alphabet are positioning for a high-stakes race to dominate the AI frontier, with Wall Street split between caution and conviction over the payoff timeline.

data.title: What’s Ahead for the Stock Market This Week
data.description:
After Friday’s sharp selloff triggered by President Trump’s decision to raise tariffs in response to China tightening rare earth exports, investors are starting the week on edge. The focus now shifts to trade tensions, earnings season, and the Federal Reserve’s next moves.

Big Earnings Week: Banks and Chipmakers Take Center Stage

This week kicks off a busy stretch of third-quarter results from major financial firms and tech companies.

  • Banks: JPMorgan Chase, Wells Fargo, Goldman Sachs, BlackRock, and Citigroup all report on Tuesday, followed by Bank of America and Morgan Stanley on Wednesday. Later in the week, American Express, Truist Financial, and State Street will wrap up the financial lineup.
  • Tech & Chips: Taiwan Semiconductor (TSMC) reports Thursday after strong AI chip sales boosted its revenue 40% in the first half of 2025. ASML follows on Wednesday after raising concerns about tariffs and slowing demand for chip equipment.
  • Tech events: Oracle’s AI World conference begins Monday, and Salesforce’s Dreamforce starts Tuesday — both likely to bring attention to artificial intelligence and enterprise tech. Earnings Calendar >>

Data Delays and Fed Watch

The federal government shutdown, now entering its third week, is likely to delay several key reports, including retail sales, inflation (PPI), housing starts, and jobless claims. Some data will still come through, like the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index on Tuesday and homebuilder confidence on Thursday.

Investors will also tune in to Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell’s speech on Tuesday, along with comments from Fed officials, including Michelle Bowman and Christopher Waller. The Fed’s Beige Book, which gives an overview of economic conditions across regions, will be released on Wednesday.

Market Context

Bond markets will be closed on Monday for Columbus Day (also observed as Indigenous Peoples Day), but stock exchanges will remain open.

With limited economic data and uncertainty around Washington’s budget standoff, markets are likely to take their cues from corporate earnings and Fed commentary.

In short, this week is about navigating through noise — balancing worries over tariffs and the shutdown against potential optimism from strong bank and chipmaker earnings.

data.title: What Is the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA)? A Complete Guide
data.description:
The Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA), often referred to as the Dow Jones, the Dow 30, or simply the Dow, is one of the world’s most recognized stock market indexes. The DJIA tracks 30 of the largest publicly traded companies in the United States, making it a widely followed indicator of the overall health of the U.S. economy and stock market. Investors searching for DJIA today, DJIA futures, or Dow Jones price updates often turn to this index as a barometer of market sentiment.

But what exactly is the DJIA, what stocks does it include, and how do traders and long-term investors use it in their strategies?

What Is the DJIA?

The Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA) is a price-weighted index created in 1896 by Charles Dow and Edward Jones. Unlike market-cap-weighted indexes like the S&P 500, the DJIA assigns weight based on a stock’s price rather than its market capitalization. This means a company with a higher stock price (e.g., Apple or UnitedHealth) influences the index more heavily than lower-priced stocks.

The DJIA is designed to reflect the performance of blue-chip companies across different sectors of the U.S. economy. It does not include utilities or transportation stocks, which are tracked by separate Dow indexes.

What Companies Are in the DJIA?

The DJIA is composed of 30 large, publicly traded U.S. companies that are considered leaders in their industries. These companies span sectors such as technology, finance, healthcare, consumer goods, and industrials.

Here’s a snapshot of the DJIA holdings (as of 2025, September 15th):

CompanyTickerSector
Apple Inc.AAPLTechnology
Microsoft Corp.MSFTTechnology
Johnson & JohnsonJNJHealthcare
UnitedHealth GroupUNHHealthcare
JPMorgan Chase & Co.JPMFinancials
Goldman Sachs GroupGSFinancials
Procter & Gamble Co.PGConsumer Staples
Coca-Cola Co.KOConsumer Staples
Boeing Co.BAIndustrials
3M CompanyMMMIndustrials
…and 20 more leading firms—Mixed sectors

Note: The index is reviewed periodically, and constituents may change depending on mergers, bankruptcies, or shifts in market relevance.

How Does the DJIA Work?

  • Price-Weighted Methodology: Higher-priced stocks have more influence.
  • Dow Divisor: A proprietary number adjusted for stock splits, dividends, and corporate actions ensures continuity.
  • Sector Balance: While tech stocks (Apple, Microsoft) now hold significant weight, the index aims to represent the broader U.S. economy.

If Apple (AAPL) rises $5 in a day, it has the same impact on the DJIA as Boeing (BA) rising $5, even though their market caps differ drastically.

DJIA vs Other Indexes

FeatureDJIA (Dow 30)S&P 500Nasdaq Composite
Companies Tracked305003,000+
Weighting MethodPrice-weightedMarket-capMarket-cap
Sector CoverageBroad (excl. utilities, transports)Broad (all sectors)Tech-heavy
Symbol^DJI^GSPC^IXIC

Why Do Investors Watch the DJIA?

  • Economic Barometer: The DJIA today is seen as a snapshot of the U.S. economy.
  • Historical Importance: Created in 1896, it has over 125 years of market history.
  • Market Sentiment: Widely quoted on news outlets and financial platforms.
  • Trading Opportunities: Investors follow DJIA futures, DJIA ETFs, and Dow Jones index funds for exposure.

How to Trade or Invest in the DJIA

Investors can’t buy the DJIA directly but can gain exposure through exchange-traded funds (ETFs), futures contracts, or mutual funds:

  • SPDR Dow Jones Industrial Average ETF (DIA) – tracks the DJIA closely.
  • DJIA Futures – contracts traded on CME, allowing leveraged exposure.
  • Index Funds – designed for long-term buy-and-hold investors.

Key Facts About the DJIA

MetricDetail
Ticker Symbol^DJI
Number of Companies30
Weighting MethodPrice-Weighted
First Published1896
Major ETFSPDR Dow Jones ETF (DIA)
Futures ExchangeChicago Mercantile Exchange

data.title: Supermicro Surges as Gross Margin Outlook Rises Amid $60B Backlog
data.description:
Shares of Supermicro (SMCI) jumped roughly 15% in premarket trading Wednesday, fueled by a robust update following Tuesday's market close. The AI server and data center equipment maker revealed it expects to nearly double its gross margins in upcoming quarters.

The company highlighted a staggering new order intake of more than $60 billion in the fourth quarter of fiscal 2026, pushing its backlog to an all-time high. Such volume underscores strong demand for Supermicro's customized server solutions amid the ongoing AI hardware surge.

Previously, Supermicro forecasted gross margins in the range of 8.2% to 8.4%. Now, it's aiming for 15% to 17%, citing favorable shifts in customer and product mix that should boost profitability significantly. This jump could signal a better pricing environment or improved operational efficiencies.

Supermicro's servers integrate chips from industry heavyweights like Nvidia, Intel, and AMD. The company recently teased big projects, including a new gigawatt-scale AI data center for SpaceX and XAI, highlighting its role in powering next-generation AI infrastructure.

Despite these tailwinds, SMCI's stock hasn't been immune to volatility. Its shares have dipped almost 13% year to date, dragged down by a June announcement of an equity raise aimed at funding the massive component purchases needed to fulfill $39 billion in AI server orders.

Supermicro's ability to convert its massive backlog into revenue and maintain its elevated margin outlook will be critical to watch as the AI hardware arms race heats up. The company's performance stands as a bellwether for how hardware suppliers are faring in the current environment.

Can Supermicro sustain this momentum and translate its backlog into consistent profits? That's the question investors will be parsing as fiscal year 2027 unfolds.

data.title: Intel's Q2 Report Poised to Reveal if AI Demand Can Sustain Its Stock Surge
data.description:
Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) is gearing up to report its second-quarter earnings on Thursday, and all eyes will be on whether the chip giant can sustain the momentum that's propelled its stock almost threefold this year. The AI frenzy has injected new life into processor demand, creating a fresh narrative around Intel's turnaround efforts that investors have eagerly embraced.

Analysts anticipate Intel to post its fastest revenue climb in roughly six years, pushed by growing demand for central processing units crucial for AI workloads. The data center and AI business segment is expected to surge around 36%, signaling that Intel's chips remain a key player in powering machine learning applications. It's a far cry from the stagnant sales the company faced in prior quarters amid a slumping PC market.

Last 4 Quarters for Intel >>

There's also buzz surrounding Intel's contract manufacturing division, which has lately gained traction with deals like a notable partnership with Tesla. The market remains curious about Intel's potential to win big-ticket clients-most notably a rumored agreement to produce processors for Apple, a pact President Donald Trump mentioned earlier this year but hasn't been officially confirmed by either party.

Securing an Apple deal would mark a significant win, boosting not only Intel's foundry revenue but also its standing as a competitive contract chipmaker challenging the likes of TSMC and Samsung. However, uncertainties linger as such a relationship hasn't yet materialized, keeping investors cautious.

Despite the rally, Intel's shares have pulled back more than 25% from their late June peak amid broader tech sell-offs, yet they still boast a staggering 185% gain year-to-date. This volatility reflects the ongoing balancing act between optimism over AI-driven growth and wariness around persistent challenges, including weaker demand for traditional PC chips and slim profit margins compared to Intel's past highs.

Bernstein analysts captured this sentiment well, noting improved confidence in Intel's outlook but flagging that fundamental headwinds remain. For instance, heavy investments in foundry capabilities and the costs tied to new manufacturing technologies continue to weigh on margins, which TD Cowen expects to come in around 38.8% this quarter.

Interestingly, Intel benefited from tight factory capacity in the last quarter, managing to sell inventory that was previously sidelined, including older and lower-spec chips. Whether that trend holds or fades could influence the upcoming performance metrics.

Market forecasts peg Intel's Q2 revenue at approximately $14.42 billion, representing a 12% year-over-year increase, with adjusted earnings around 21 cents per share. The data center and AI segment alone is expected to generate $5.37 billion in sales, underscoring the significant role these newer growth areas are playing amidst a transforming chip landscape.

What will be revealing is if Intel's numbers confirm that AI fueling is sturdy enough to offset legacy business pressures. The company's ability to keep expanding its foundry services and secure marquee partnerships might shape its narrative well beyond this earnings call.

data.title: Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley Shine in Q2, Analysts Eye European Opportunities
data.description:
Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley have just wrapped up a blockbuster second quarter, showing strength across equities, investment banking, and wealth management. This powerhouse performance has grabbed attention, sparking upgraded earnings forecasts from analysts watching Wall Street closely.

HSBC's U.S. financial research team, under Saul Martinez, upped their earnings-per-share estimates for both banks. Goldman Sachs in particular got a notable boost, with HSBC hiking its price target from $834 to $995 and lifting its rating from reduce to hold. That's quite a bit of renewed confidence in the firm's road ahead.

These upgrades didn't come out of nowhere. The first half of the year displayed robust gains that outpaced many expectations, emphasizing the banks' ability to profit despite a complicated market backdrop. The investment banking segment especially proved resilient, riding on a resurgence of deal activity and equity trading.

But it's not just the U.S. financial titans catching attention. Analysts are also speculating that European counterparts might soon feel those tailwinds. With similar trends in equity markets and deal flow, major European banks could be poised for replay of this success.

Goldman Sachs CEO David Solomon has overseen a string of strong quarters, cementing the bank's status as a leader in wealth management and capital markets. Morgan Stanley's diversification across wealth management and advisory businesses has likewise cushioned it in volatile trading environments.

That said, the gains raise some interesting questions about the sustainability of such momentum. With economic headwinds and regulatory landscapes shifting, the big-name banks will need to keep adapting to maintain this run - or risk giving back some of these impressive gains.

Meanwhile, investors and analysts alike are watching the potential ripple effects across the financial sector globally. Could the model that worked for Goldman and Morgan Stanley continue feeding into strong quarters for their peers across the pond? Time and market moves will tell.

One thing's for sure: the spotlight on earnings and valuation shifts in these banking giants has never been sharper.

data.title: July 22: Tech Titans Brace for Earnings as Oil Prices Surge on Middle East Tensions
data.description:
Futures tied to major U.S. stock indexes slipped slightly early Wednesday as traders awaited a flood of tech earnings reports that could clarify the direction of artificial intelligence investment. The spotlight is on Alphabet and Texas Instruments, while Tesla prepares to post figures expected to reveal a rare quarterly cash drain tied to its AI ambitions.

Despite this cautious start, Wall Street's main indexes had gained ground the previous session, driven largely by strength in semiconductor stocks. The Philadelphia semiconductor index jumped over 5%, marking its best performance in a month, highlighting the market's focus on the chipmakers powering AI development.

All eyes are on Alphabet's earnings release scheduled after today's market close. Analysts are betting that the tech giant's results, especially regarding its AI investments and capital spend, will influence investor sentiment across the broader sector. Industry watchers note that although Alphabet is planning a huge capital expenditure, estimates anticipate its 2026 budget could rise by roughly 5% to between $190 billion and $200 billion amid an accelerating AI demand and steady memory chip price hikes.

Google Earnings History >>

Wall Street projections via BofA Securities peg Alphabet's second-quarter sales at around $101 billion with earnings per share near $2.90. While some investors worry about the return timeline on these significant AI outlays, strategists remain optimistic about the company's potential for outsized growth and improved cloud margins driven by favorable AI positioning.

Texas Instruments steps into the spotlight as a bellwether for chip demand. The firm's forecasts indicate second-quarter revenue in the range of $5 billion to $5.4 billion and earnings per share between $1.77 and $2.05. Given TI's role as a leading producer of analog chips, its results offer valuable insight into the health of the chip sector, which is a major driver of expected earnings growth for the S&P 500 this quarter-chipmakers could contribute almost half of the total earnings gains.

Tesla is expected to report its first quarterly net cash burn in over two years, a sign of the hefty investments CEO Elon Musk is making in AI and robotics. With a pivot away from traditional electric vehicles, Tesla's outlook now hinges on breakthroughs in self-driving taxis and humanoid robots. Analysts remain wary as Musk's ambitious timelines have slipped, and Tesla's stock has declined more than 13% this year amidst uncertainty about the company's strategic reorientation.

On the commodities front, oil is climbing to its highest levels in nearly six weeks. Tensions in the Middle East have escalated, with U.S. airstrikes targeting Iranian military assets for an 11th consecutive night. These developments, coupled with a naval blockade affecting Saudi Arabia, are fueling supply concerns. Benchmark Brent crude futures jumped 3% to about $93.73, while U.S. West Texas Intermediate rose to nearly $86.91 per barrel-both marking their fourth straight daily advance.

The geopolitical friction is shifting how markets price risk in oil, suggesting that any flare-up could quickly push prices higher, raising the stakes for energy markets worldwide.

As Alphabet leads a busy earnings week, and oil markets tighten on geopolitical fragility, traders have plenty to chew on in the coming days.

data.title: Airbus Shares Surge 7% Following €5 Billion Buyback Announcement and Ambitious 2029 EBIT Goal
data.description:
Airbus SE (PAR:AIR) shares leapt 7% on Wednesday, propelled by the aerospace giant's announcement of a hefty €5 billion share buyback program scheduled over the next three years. The move coincided with the unveiling of an ambitious adjusted EBIT goal of between €12 billion and €13 billion by 2029.

During a London business update, CEO Guillaume Faury outlined the company's growth and financial strategy. Alongside leaders from Commercial Aircraft, Helicopters, and Defence & Space divisions, he illustrated Airbus's plans to capitalize on strong market demand with a focus on innovative and sustainable aerospace products.

The buyback program, greenlit by Airbus's board, awaits ongoing shareholder approval but signals management's commitment to returning value. Crucially, the €12-13 billion EBIT target assumes an exchange rate of 1.22 euros per dollar and maintains a cash conversion target near 100% over five years, with no change to the existing 2026 outlook.

Faury emphasized the group's dedication to profitable expansion across all business segments to meet heightened demand for civil and military aerospace solutions, underpinning ongoing investments and innovation. The outlook rests on stable global trade conditions, air traffic growth, and predictable supply chain operations.

Analysts at Morgan Stanley (NYSE:MS) described the update as notably upbeat compared to previous expectations. They flagged the share buyback as a significant surprise, estimating that it corresponds to around 1% of the company's market cap annually and up to 25% of cumulative free cash flow from 2026 through 2028.

The brokerage's assessment anticipates Airbus will return approximately 60% of its cumulative free cash flow to shareholders across dividends and buybacks over the next three years. They further detailed industry segment EBIT targets: Commercial Aircraft around €10 billion, Defence & Space €1.3 billion, and Helicopters €1.2 billion.

Discussions also revealed a production rate adjustment for the A320 aircraft, expecting 70 to 75 units per month by 2027. Previous communications hinted at a steady 75 units monthly beyond 2027, but Airbus stopped short of reaffirming that pace going forward.

All in all, the move demonstrates Airbus's confidence in a recovery and growth trajectory more robust than many had factored in, even as it navigates a complex aerospace market with external uncertainties. The next few years appear pivotal for translating this renewed momentum into tangible financial results.

data.title: Michael Burry Reduces Oracle Short Position but Keeps Bet Alive Amid Stock Slide
data.description:
Michael Burry is keeping his foot on the gas with Oracle (ORCL), despite reports that he'd exited his bearish wager. On July 18, Burry took to X to squash rumors, clarifying he'd only trimmed half of his January 2027 put options. "This is fake news. I covered half because winning," he wrote, making it clear his bet against Oracle remains firmly in place.

The software giant's shares hit a fresh 52-week low at $121.50 that same day, a dramatic plunge from its all-time high near $346. That 63% drop has turned Burry's short position into one of the headline-making moves on Wall Street this year, given the sheer scale of the decline.

In more detail, Burry revealed on Substack that he scaled back because the position got too large to hold comfortably, not because he lost conviction. He was candid about avoiding attachment to winning option trades and emphasized he's kept half of his exposure intact, signaling ongoing skepticism about Oracle's near-term performance.

Burry initially lifted the curtain on his Oracle put options in early 2026 shortly after winding down his Scion Asset Management hedge fund. His bearish thesis pivots around Oracle's ramped-up AI infrastructure spending and risky customer concentration, especially its reliance on OpenAI. Institutional players have echoed these concerns: S&P Global recently downgraded Oracle's credit rating to BBB-, perilously close to junk status, highlighting OpenAI's outsized role in the company's $638 billion backlog and a predicted negative free cash flow of $42 billion in fiscal 2027.

The credit rating downgrade puts a spotlight on Oracle's financial pressures, transforming a qualitative red flag into a stark quantitative reality. Meanwhile, Burry's update seems to have sparked some confusion among retail traders. Data from Stocktwits, analyzed by Yahoo Finance, shows an "extremely bullish" sentiment spike on Oracle after his X post, despite Burry's sustained short stance-a fascinating divergence between his outlook and retail enthusiasm.

Burry's Oracle shorts aren't his only move. He's also holding puts on Nvidia and had already trimmed his Palantir short by mid-2026. Still, Oracle remains one of his most visible and, judging by the stock price plunge, his most lucrative plays so far.

Oracle isn't out of the woods yet. The company has flagged plans to issue $20 billion in new equity later this year, which risks diluting shareholders at current depressed prices far below last year's highs. Coupled with the lingering credit rating pressure, this could weigh heavily on the stock in coming quarters. If that scenario unfolds, the remaining half of Burry's puts will have some fresh runway to possibly run.

Oracle hasn't publicly addressed Burry's ongoing short position or the downgrade saga as it braces for its next earnings update, leaving plenty of room for speculation on how these factors will play out in the near term.
